does: This paper develops a thermodynamic framework for AI alignment by introducing ethical entropy—a measure of goal divergence in autonomous systems—and proves that without active alignment work, this entropy increases over time in gradient-based optimizers. The authors define entropy formally as S = -Σ p(g_i; theta) ln p(g_i; theta) over a finite set of intended goals and derive a critical stability boundary γ_crit = (λ_max / 2) ln N, where λ_max is the dominant eigenvalue of the Fisher Information Matrix and N is the number of model parameters. Empirical validation on a 7-billion-parameter model demonstrates that unregularized systems drift significantly in entropy while properly regularized systems with alignment work γ = 1.5γ_crit maintain stable entropy near zero, establishing a quantitative relationship between alignment effort and system stability.
connects: This work extends the analogy between physical thermodynamics and machine learning dynamics, building on prior research treating optimization and generalization through entropic lenses. It connects to the broader AI alignment literature that grapples with specification gaming, reward hacking, and goal drift—problems intensified by exploration noise in large-scale optimizers. The paper draws on information-theoretic approaches to understanding neural network behavior and Fisher Information Matrix analysis for characterizing optimization landscapes. It complements existing alignment strategies by providing a formal, quantitative framework that unifies previously disparate observations about why unaligned systems fail, relating to gradient-based learning dynamics in ways that echo work on loss landscape geometry and implicit regularization.
enables: This framework establishes a quantitative foundation for alignment work requirements as systems scale, suggesting that alignment costs grow logarithmically with parameter count—a potentially tractable scaling relationship. The theory enables prediction of critical points where systems begin manifesting unintended behaviors and provides metrics for evaluating alignment interventions. Future work can leverage this formalism to design provably stable optimization procedures that maintain entropy bounds during training, develop better regularization schemes parameterized by system size, and extend the framework to non-gradient-based learners. The thermodynamic perspective opens possibilities for continuous monitoring of alignment health during deployment and could inform hardware-level safeguards that enforce entropy constraints, establishing AI safety as a control problem with physical foundations.
